does: This paper introduces TEVI, a framework that improves vision-language alignment in models like CLIP by selectively editing image embeddings based on text captions. The core contribution uses sparse autoencoders to decompose image embeddings into interpretable features, then trains a masking module that reconstructs embeddings to contain only information described by accompanying captions. The authors evaluate TEVI on controlled synthetic captions to validate that it preserves caption-relevant attributes while removing extraneous information, then apply it to CLIP models on standard benchmarks (MS COCO, Flickr, IIW, DOCCI, RoCOCO), demonstrating consistent improvements in retrieval performance with larger gains on datasets with richer, longer captions.
connects: This work builds directly on observations that vision-language models suffer from information imbalance, where images contain substantially more information than their text descriptions capture, limiting alignment quality. It extends prior research on understanding CLIP embedding spaces and their limitations for multimodal tasks. The use of sparse autoencoders draws on recent advances in mechanistic interpretability that decompose high-dimensional embeddings into sparse, interpretable components. TEVI responds to the broader challenge in vision-language alignment of handling heterogeneous information across modalities and relates to work on embedding refinement, contrastive learning improvements, and caption-guided vision processing.
enables: This framework opens several research directions for improving vision-language model alignment. It suggests that sparse autoencoders could enable more fine-grained control over embedding properties beyond caption alignment, such as removing spurious correlations or enhancing specific attributes for downstream tasks. The approach could extend to other vision-language models beyond CLIP or be adapted for other modality pairs. Future work might explore applying similar masking strategies during training rather than post-hoc, scaling to higher-capacity sparse autoencoders, or using TEVI for data cleaning and curation. The interpretability gained from sparse decomposition could also support debugging and understanding failure modes in vision-language systems.
